result:
I.

The rain came sideways that afternoon, the kind of rain that doesn’t fall so much as migrate. I sat on the narrow covered porch of a rented cottage on the west coast of Ireland, watching it lash the fuchsia bushes until their purple bells looked like tiny drowned lanterns. A mug of tea had gone cold in my hands. I hadn’t taken a sip in twenty minutes. I was thinking about silence.

Not the absence of sound. Real silence is rare and expensive; you have to buy it with distance and weather and the decision to stop narrating your own life for five consecutive minutes. What I had instead was the sound of everything that wasn’t human: wind worrying the slates, the low electric hum of the Atlantic a quarter-mile away, the occasional wet slap of a gull fighting the gusts. It felt like the planet had taken the microphone away from our species for a moment and was letting us listen to its older, slower thoughts.

I had come here to disappear on purpose. Not in the dramatic, Instagram-breakup kind of way. I simply wanted to see what would happen if I stopped feeding the machine that turns experience into content. No posting. No notes app soliloquies. No “this is what I’m learning” performance. Just weather, tea, books, and the stubborn project of being a person without an audience.

It was harder than I expected. The first three days felt like withdrawal. My thumbs kept hovering over phantom keyboards. My mind kept drafting tweets about how quiet it was. The ego is a stubborn tenant; it doesn’t leave just because you change the locks.

But something shifted on the fourth morning. I woke before dawn and instead of reaching for my phone I reached for a sweater and went outside. The sky was the color of wet slate. A single heron stood in the tidal pool like a question mark someone had forgotten to erase. I stood there long enough for my feet to go numb inside my boots and felt, for maybe the first time in years, that I was exactly where I was. Not performing the moment. Not storing it for later. Just…here.

That small click—like a latch finding its home—opened everything.

II.

I started walking.

Not ambitious hikes with crampons and existential crises. Just putting one foot in front of the other along the cliffs, through the bog, past the ruined famine cottages that look like broken teeth in the landscape. Ireland is a country that remembers everything and forgives nothing. Every stone wall has a story about who built it and who was forced to leave it. The grass grows over atrocity with a kind of merciless tenderness. Life goes on, it seems to say, but it refuses to pretend the dead weren’t here.

I thought a lot about memory during those walks. Not personal memory, though that crept in too. Collective memory. The way certain landscapes hold trauma in their very shape. How the Atlantic looks the same as it did when coffin ships left these shores carrying people who would never see land again. The sea doesn’t apologize. It just keeps moving, blue and pitiless and beautiful.

On the fifth day I found a stone that looked exactly like a sleeping dog. I sat on it and cried for no reason I could name. The tears felt ancient, like they’d been waiting for me in some aquifer beneath the peat. When they stopped I felt lighter, the way you do after vomiting up something poisonous you didn’t even know you’d swallowed.

That afternoon I started writing by hand for the first time in years. Not typing. Actual pen on actual paper. The physical act felt like using a muscle I’d let atrophy. My handwriting looked like a stranger’s. Childish loops and uncertain pressure. I didn’t write anything important. Just descriptions of what I could see: the particular way rain beaded on gorse, the sound a plastic bag makes when the wind gets inside it and it tries to become a bird, the smell of turf smoke coming from someone’s chimney half a mile away.

Writing without the intention to show anyone is a completely different neurological event than writing for an audience. It’s slower. More honest. More stupid. I wrote sentences that would have made me wince if I’d imagined anyone reading them. Things like “the sky is the color of a bruise that’s starting to heal.” Embarrassing. Indulgent. True.

III.

On the seventh day the rain stopped and the wind dropped and the stars came out so bright they looked fake. I lay on my back in the grass behind the cottage with a bottle of cheap Irish whiskey and tried to remember the names of constellations. I could only get Orion and the Plough. Everything else was just generous handfuls of salt thrown across black velvet.

I thought about how small we are and how that smallness should be comforting rather than terrifying. Most of our suffering comes from pretending we’re the main character. The universe has no idea who you are, and it’s doing just fine. There’s freedom in that. You can stop performing. You can fail quietly. You can love people without needing them to complete some narrative arc you’ve written for yourself.

I got very drunk and told the stars my worst secrets. They did not seem impressed. One of them winked, or maybe that was just the whiskey.

The next morning I woke up with a headache and a strange certainty that I had never really been in love. Not the capital-L kind. I had been attached. I had been infatuated. I had mistaken chemistry for destiny and convenience for compatibility. But real love—the kind that rearranges your molecules—had always seemed like something that happened to other, braver people.

This realization should have been depressing. Instead it felt like being handed back the keys to my own life. If I had never really loved, then I had never really lost. The past could be a country I was finally allowed to leave.

I made coffee and burned the toast and didn’t care. The gulls were screaming their ridiculous pterodactyl screams. The sea was doing its thing. Everything was fine.

IV.

Time started behaving differently. Days stretched and compressed like taffy. I would spend six hours reading a single book about moss (yes, there are entire books about moss, and they are better than most novels). I would spend thirty minutes watching a spider repair its web after the rain and feel that I had witnessed something sacred.

I became slightly feral. My beard grew in weird patches. I stopped matching my socks. I talked to the ravens that hung around the cottage like delinquent teenagers and they talked back in their hoarse, sarcastic voices. One of them started leaving me gifts: a button, a piece of sea glass, once what I’m almost certain was a human tooth. I didn’t ask questions.

At night I listened to the Shipping Forecast on an ancient transistor radio. The poetry of it killed me. “Dogger, Fisher, German Bight. Northeast gale force 8, veering cyclonic, severe gale 9 later.” It sounded like the names of Norse gods fighting in the dark. I fell asleep to predictions of weather I would never see, in places I would never visit, and felt mysteriously anchored by that fact.

The cottage had no television and terrible internet. This was by design. Without the constant drip of other people’s opinions, my own opinions started showing up again. They were shyer than I remembered. They had opinions about silence and rain and the particular melancholy of abandoned stone piers. They thought maybe consciousness itself was a kind of weather system—patterns moving through meat for reasons the meat would never fully understand.

I wrote a lot of bad poetry. Then I wrote some that wasn’t completely terrible. Then I burned all of it in the little fireplace because the act of burning felt more honest than the act of keeping. The smoke smelled like lavender and regret. I slept better that night than I had in months.

V.

One afternoon I walked further than usual and found myself at the edge of a place where the land simply gave up and fell into the sea. The cliffs were two hundred feet high. Below, the water was the color of a bottle you’d find in an antique shop—deep green with veins of turquoise. The wind was so strong it felt like it was trying to push me into the next life.

I lay down on my belly and crawled to the very edge because I am occasionally an idiot. Looking straight down was like looking into God’s basement. The vertigo was so complete it circled around and became a kind of peace. For one clear moment I understood why people jump. Not because they want to die but because the edge is honest in a way the rest of life isn’t. The edge doesn’t negotiate. It doesn’t ghost you or love you conditionally or waste your time with small talk. The edge just is.

I backed away slowly, heart hammering, and sat among the thrift and sea campion with shaking hands. A bee landed on my knee and cleaned its antennae with fastidious care. The contrast between the abyss and the bee felt like the entire human condition in miniature. We are always one small decision away from eternity and one small creature away from tenderness. Both are true at the same time. The trick is remembering that.

That night I dreamed I was a lighthouse. Not the keeper. The building itself. I stood on a rock in the middle of nowhere sending my stupid yellow light into the void on schedule, whether anyone needed it or not. I woke up crying again, but this time the tears felt like they came from the future instead of the past.

VI.

I started thinking about returning to the world the way astronauts must think about coming back from space. The gravity would be different. The air would be crowded. People would expect me to make sense.

What had I learned, exactly? That silence is edible if you’re hungry enough for it. That time is mostly a rumor we agree to believe. That love might be less like finding the right person and more like becoming the right weather system—capable of holding both storm and calm in the same sky without needing to choose.

I learned that most of what I called thinking was actually just narrating. That most of what I called feeling was actually just performing feelings I thought I should have. That the self is mostly friction—the resistance generated when an ape with anxiety disorder tries to pretend it understands what’s happening.

I learned that the world is dying and also being born at the same time, and both processes are so much larger than us that our most noble and terrible acts are basically microscopic. This is not nihilism. This is mercy. You are off the hook in ways you never dared to dream.

I also learned that Irish butter is criminally underrated and that ravens remember your face and that if you leave your whiskey out overnight the fairy folk will drink the top inch and replace it with something that tastes like moonlight and poor decisions.

VII.

On my last full day the weather turned strange. Fog rolled in so thick I couldn’t see the end of my own arm. The world became a watercolor left out in the rain. Every sound was muffled and important. I could hear my own heartbeat like it was coming from another room.

I walked down to the small beach anyway. The tide was out. The sand was the color of wet cement and covered in the looping signatures of worms. I took off my boots and walked until the cold made my teeth hurt. Then I stood there while the fog moved around me like it was trying to tell me something in a language I almost understood.

A seal popped its head up twenty feet away and regarded me with ancient, slightly judgmental eyes. We looked at each other for a long time. Two mammals who had wandered into each other’s element by accident. I don’t know what it saw when it looked at me. Probably an incompetent hairless seal who had forgotten how to swim properly. I saw something that had never once needed therapy or social media or the approval of strangers. The seal seemed complete in a way I have never been. It made a sound like a disappointed grandfather and disappeared back into the gray.

I laughed until I couldn’t breathe. The fog swallowed the sound immediately, like it was embarrassed for me.

That night I packed my small bag. I left half the whiskey for the next lost person who rented the cottage. I left a note that just said “Thank you” because anything more would have been vulgar. Then I sat on the porch one last time and listened to the dark.

VIII.

The next morning the taxi came at dawn. The driver was a man named Seamus who told me that his grandmother could cure warts by rubbing them with a dead cat. I believed him. The Irish understand that reality is mostly negotiable.

As we drove away from the coast the sun came out and turned the mountains into emeralds. I felt something like grief and something like gratitude having an argument in my chest. The grief was for the version of me that had arrived here—nervous, clenched, performing even when no one was watching. The gratitude was for the version leaving—slightly more porous, less certain, carrying the knowledge that silence is not empty but full of everything we’re usually too loud to hear.

I don’t know if any of this will stick. The world has a way of reasserting its patterns. Already I can feel the old habits licking their chops at the edges of my mind. The need to be interesting. The compulsion to translate lived experience into digestible narrative. The small betrayals we commit against our own attention every day.

But something shifted that can’t be completely un-shifted. I tasted a different way of being alive. Slower. Quieter. More astonished. I met the edge and didn’t jump. I met the bee and didn’t crush it. I met myself without the usual choreography and discovered that the choreography had been hiding most of the interesting moves.

IX.

The plane lifted out of Shannon Airport and I watched Ireland fall away beneath me like a dream you’re already starting to forget. The fields looked like they’d been embroidered by someone with too much time and too much sorrow. I ordered a whiskey at 9:17 in the morning because fuck it, I was between worlds.

The flight attendant had kind eyes and a tattoo of a small bird escaping from a cage just visible above her collar. I wanted to tell her everything I’d learned but understood that would be insane. Instead I just said thank you when she brought my drink. She smiled like she knew anyway.

Somewhere over the Atlantic I fell asleep and dreamed I was the rain. I was falling through myself toward the place where all rivers begin. It was neither happy nor sad. It was just weather doing what weather does.

When I woke up we were beginning our descent into reality. The seatbelt sign dinged. The cabin lights came up. My fellow passengers started performing consciousness again—checking phones, fixing hair, assembling the faces they would show customs.

I looked out the window at the continent below and felt the familiar gravity of it all. Meetings. Responsibilities. The endless conversation we’re all having with ourselves about whether we’re doing life correctly.

But in my pocket was a small piece of sea glass the color of nothing that exists on land. The raven’s final gift. I ran my thumb over its smooth edges and remembered the sound the wind made through the stones of an abandoned cottage at dusk. I remembered what it felt like to have no reception, no agenda, no witness.

The self is mostly a story we tell to keep the silence at bay. But sometimes, if you’re very lucky and very still, the silence tells you its own story back. It has no moral and no plot. It just says: this. This is enough. This rain. This breath. This small honest moment between eternities.

I closed my fist around the sea glass and smiled at no one in particular.

The plane touched down with a gentle bump. Welcome home, the tires seemed to say. Or maybe welcome to whatever comes next.

Either way, I was ready to begin again.

(Word count: 2498)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
